name: pecan
shortDescription: A pecan with a ridged shell and a rich sweet kernel hidden inside, prized by bakers, travelers, and anyone patient enough to crack it properly. It keeps well in sacks and turns plain porridge or pastry into something that tastes like a better season.
flavorText: “Pecans are little wooden chests with dessert locked inside.”

name: pisatcchio
shortDescription: A small split-shelled pistachio, misspelled on more than one dock ledger but recognized instantly by hungry buyers. Its pale green nut is salty, costly by the handful, and fashionable enough that nobles forgive the trouble of opening it.
flavorText: “A pistachio is the only nut that smiles before being eaten.”

name: walnut
shortDescription: A walnut with a hard wrinkled shell and an oily, earthy kernel useful for breads, sauces, sweets, and winter stores. It looks like a tiny brain, which has inspired both healer lectures and very poor tavern jokes.
flavorText: “Walnuts teach the same lesson as trade: the valuable part is rarely on the outside.”

name: cashew
shortDescription: A curved cashew with a creamy bite and a price that reminds buyers it traveled farther than common nuts. Cooks grind it into sauces, confectioners hide it in sweet cakes, and merchants like how little space such luxury occupies.
flavorText: “A cashew is shaped like a question because no one agrees how many are enough.”

name: anchovy
shortDescription: A small anchovy, silver and sharp-scented, valued more for flavor than size. Salted, pressed, or chopped into sauces, it can make an entire pot taste deeper while remaining small enough for proud diners to deny noticing.
flavorText: “The smallest fish often has the loudest opinion.”

name: bass
shortDescription: A fresh bass with firm white flesh, clean scales, and a respectable shape for market display. It sells well to cooks who want river flavor without too many bones, and to inns that know a whole fish looks finer than a fillet.
flavorText: “Bass are honest fish, which is more than can be said for most anglers.”

name: calamari
shortDescription: Cleaned calamari ready for the pan, pale, tender, and quick to turn from delicacy to boot leather in careless hands. Coastal cooks prize it for frying, stuffing, and stews that smell like harbor markets at dawn.
flavorText: “Calamari rewards the cook who knows when to stop.”

name: carp
shortDescription: A sturdy carp with broad scales and firm flesh, common in ponds, river traps, and village markets. It is not glamorous, but it feeds families reliably and gives patient cooks plenty to work with once the bones are respected.
flavorText: “Carp survives in muddy water and gossip, which makes it almost a merchant.”

name: catfish
shortDescription: A whiskered catfish with slick skin and rich flesh, often pulled from slow rivers and deep muddy pools. Buyers judge it by smell, size, and the confidence of the fisher, because catfish has a way of keeping secrets.
flavorText: “Never trust a fish with whiskers, but season it well.”

name: cod
shortDescription: A clean cod with pale flakes and a mild flavor that travels well when salted or dried. It is the backbone of ports, ship stores, and humble kitchens, turning distance into supper for people far from the sea.
flavorText: “Cod is the fish that taught sailors to believe in tomorrow.”

name: crab
shortDescription: A crab with a hard shell, sharp claws, and sweet meat hidden behind more effort than impatient diners prefer. It sells best near the coast, though inland nobles pay handsomely for the pleasure of making a mess politely.
flavorText: “Crab is proof that treasure often pinches back.”

name: Tuna
shortDescription: A heavy tuna with dense red flesh and the market presence of a fish that could feed a whole crew. It spoils quickly without care, but when handled well it commands coin from taverns, smokehouses, and coastal halls alike.
flavorText: “Some fish arrive like cargo. Tuna arrives like an event.”

name: flounder
shortDescription: A flat flounder with delicate flesh and the peculiar look of a fish built by a distracted river spirit. Cooks like it for quick pans and gentle sauces, while merchants like how unusual shape makes buyers remember it.
flavorText: “Flounder proves that the sea has a sense of humor and a good pantry.”

name: haddock
shortDescription: A haddock with pale flaky meat and a clean sea scent, excellent for smoking, frying, or thick chowders. It is a practical fish with enough character to matter, especially in cold towns where warm bowls sell better than speeches.
flavorText: “Haddock is cod’s livelier cousin, and knows it.”

name: herring
shortDescription: A small herring with bright scales and an oily richness that made entire ports depend on its season. Salted in barrels or sold fresh at dawn, it feeds sailors, workers, and anyone willing to smell like breakfast afterward.
flavorText: “Where herring runs, ledgers grow fat.”

name: lobster
shortDescription: A lobster with armored claws, a curled tail, and meat sweet enough to make inland buyers forget the journey. It is awkward to pack, dramatic to serve, and expensive enough that taverns announce it before cooking it.
flavorText: “Lobster wears armor because dinner is a battle it usually loses.”

name: mussel
shortDescription: A mussel in a dark shell, humble by itself and splendid by the potful. It opens to steam, wine, and herbs, feeding dockhands cheaply or noble guests stylishly depending entirely on the bowl it lands in.
flavorText: “Mussels are poor food until someone charges rich people for the broth.”

name: oysters
shortDescription: Fresh oysters in rough shells, briny, delicate, and risky enough to make careful merchants check the tide, the ice, and the seller twice. They are eaten quickly, praised loudly, and blamed even louder when handled badly.
flavorText: “An oyster is a secret the sea keeps in a stone cup.”

name: perch
shortDescription: A perch with neat stripes, firm flesh, and the friendly size of fish sold by baskets rather than boasts. Village cooks fry them crisp, children recognize them from riverbanks, and market buyers trust them when they look clear-eyed.
flavorText: “Perch is the fish of quiet ponds and loud frying pans.”

name: salmon
shortDescription: A salmon with rich orange flesh and the respected reputation of a fish that fights rivers before meeting the knife. Fresh, smoked, or salted, it carries both flavor and status, which is why merchants protect it like coin.
flavorText: “Salmon swims upstream, then somehow costs more downstream.”

name: scallop
shortDescription: A scallop with a ridged shell and a sweet round morsel inside, prized by cooks who can afford delicacy. It sears quickly, spoils quickly, and sells quickly when presented to buyers who enjoy small luxuries with large prices.
flavorText: “Scallops are sea coins that cannot be spent twice.”

name: tilapia
shortDescription: A mild tilapia with clean flesh and a forgiving flavor, raised in ponds where reliable food matters more than romance. It is easy to cook, easy to sell, and unlikely to frighten customers who distrust strong fish.
flavorText: “Tilapia never brags, which is why practical cooks keep buying it.”

name: trout
shortDescription: A trout with speckled skin and sweet river flesh, best sold fresh before its brightness fades. It belongs to clear streams, mountain inns, and suppers where the cook wants the plate to taste like cold water and smoke.
flavorText: “A trout is a river’s silver signature.”

name: walleye
shortDescription: A walleye with glassy eyes, pale flesh, and a reputation among fishers who swear it tastes best because it makes them work for it. It sells well in inland markets where lake fish outrank sea tales.
flavorText: “Walleye watches the hook like it has read the contract.”

name: beets
shortDescription: A bunch of beets with dark red roots, leafy tops, and enough color to stain fingers, boards, and suspicious aprons. They are earthy, filling, and useful for soups, pickles, dyes, and dramatic kitchen mistakes.
flavorText: “Beets make every cook look guilty.”

name: broccoli
shortDescription: A head of broccoli with tight green crowns and sturdy stalks, valued by cooks who need freshness that can stand up to heat. It is healthier than it is popular with children, which somehow improves its reputation among healers.
flavorText: “Broccoli is a tiny forest that somehow found its way into stew.”

name: brussels sprouts
shortDescription: Brussels sprouts clustered like miniature cabbages, dense, green, and capable of dividing a dining hall into loyal factions. Roasted well they are sweet and hearty; boiled badly they become evidence against the cook.
flavorText: “Small cabbages have large opinions.”

name: cabbage
shortDescription: A heavy cabbage with layered leaves and the stubborn virtue of food that stores through hard weather. It becomes slaw, stew, pickle, or dumpling filling, making it one of the quiet pillars of poor kitchens and rich armies.
flavorText: “Cabbage is what winter eats when it wants to survive itself.”

name: carrot
shortDescription: A carrot with bright orange flesh, green tops, and the honest sweetness of root-cellar food. It sells by the bunch to cooks, stables, and mothers who insist that seeing better is worth chewing something crunchy.
flavorText: “Carrots improve vision, especially when spotting profit in soup.”

name: cauliflower
shortDescription: A cauliflower with pale curds tucked under green leaves, mild in flavor but handsome enough for careful market display. It soaks up butter, spice, and status, becoming whatever the kitchen can afford to make of it.
flavorText: “Cauliflower is cabbage dressed for a white banquet.”

name: celery
shortDescription: A bundle of celery with crisp ribs, leafy tops, and a clean bite that brightens soups, stocks, and travel rations. It is rarely the hero of a dish, but many dishes fail quietly when it is missing.
flavorText: “Celery is the sound of freshness pretending to be a vegetable.”

name: corn
shortDescription: An ear of corn with golden kernels packed tight along the cob, sweet when fresh and useful when dried or ground. It feeds markets, animals, and armies alike, which makes it less humble than it first appears.
flavorText: “Corn counts its wealth one kernel at a time.”

name: cucumber
shortDescription: A cool cucumber with green skin, pale flesh, and enough water inside to refresh a dusty traveler. It sells fresh for salads, salted for pickles, and sliced thin by inns trying to make simple plates look refined.
flavorText: “A cucumber is a drink that learned to crunch.”

name: eggplant
shortDescription: A glossy eggplant with purple skin and soft flesh that drinks oil, smoke, and spice with alarming enthusiasm. It looks luxurious on the stall, cooks down into comfort, and confuses buyers expecting eggs from the name.
flavorText: “Eggplant is proof that names can be wrong and dinner can still be right.”

name: garlic
shortDescription: A bulb of garlic with papery skin and cloves sharp enough to rescue stew, frighten illness, and offend delicate company. It stores well, sells steadily, and makes even poor kitchens smell like they have a plan.
flavorText: “Garlic is the smallest weapon in the cook’s arsenal.”

name: green beans
shortDescription: Green beans bundled crisp and slender, quick to cook and easy to trade in baskets. They bring color to dull meals, snap under the knife, and remind buyers that not every useful vegetable needs to be a root.
flavorText: “Green beans are what gardens whisper when they are feeling generous.”

name: lettuce
shortDescription: A head of lettuce with fresh leaves and a short temper for heat, best sold early before the market sun steals its charm. It is light food, but useful when heavy tables need something green enough to pretend at balance.
flavorText: “Lettuce wilts faster than courage in tax season.”

name: leeks
shortDescription: Leeks with white stalks, green tops, and a gentler onion sweetness that suits soups, pies, and careful sauces. They carry dirt between their layers, so good cooks wash them twice and bad cooks learn why.
flavorText: “A leek is an onion that went to finishing school and kept muddy boots.”

name: onions
shortDescription: Onions with papery skins and sharp layers, essential to nearly every kitchen that owns a knife and a pot. They keep well, sell constantly, and make cooks cry before making customers grateful.
flavorText: “Onions prove that grief and flavor can share a basket.”

name: parsnips
shortDescription: Parsnips with pale roots and a sweet earthiness that deepens in cold weather. They roast well, thicken stews, and offer a reliable winter sweetness when fruit is gone and honey is being guarded like treasure.
flavorText: “Parsnips are carrots that learned patience from frost.”

name: peas
shortDescription: Peas tucked in green pods, small, sweet, and quick to lose charm after picking. Fresh they brighten plates, dried they feed long winters, and either way they teach merchants that tiny goods can fill heavy sacks.
flavorText: “Peas are little green coins paid by the garden.”

name: radishes
shortDescription: Radishes with red skins, white flesh, and a peppery snap that wakes dull meals immediately. They grow fast, sell in cheerful bundles, and give poor tables a bite of excitement without needing an expensive spice chest.
flavorText: “Radishes are garden sparks with leaves attached.”

name: red pepper
shortDescription: A red pepper with glossy skin, crisp flesh, and a sweet heat that brightens both stew and stall. It is colorful enough to draw eyes from across the market and useful enough to justify the attention.
flavorText: “A red pepper is a lantern the kitchen can eat.”

name: potatoes
shortDescription: Potatoes with rough skins and dependable weight, the sort of food that fills bellies without demanding praise. They boil, roast, mash, fry, and forgive neglect, which makes them dear to soldiers, inns, and large families.
flavorText: “Potatoes are humble until hunger counts them.”

name: turnips
shortDescription: Turnips with pale bulbs and purple shoulders, sturdy enough for cellars and honest enough for poor pots. They are not glamorous, but they survive cold, stretch stews, and keep villages fed when finer crops become stories.
flavorText: “A turnip never asks to be loved, only useful.”

name: zucchini
shortDescription: A green zucchini with tender flesh and a habit of appearing in markets faster than cooks can invent recipes. It grills, stews, stuffs, and hides in bread, making abundance look like strategy.
flavorText: “Zucchini is what happens when a garden gets carried away.”

name: lentils
shortDescription: Lentils in small dry rounds, modest in appearance and powerful in the pot. They store for ages, cook into filling stews, and make merchants respect any food that can feed a crowd from a surprisingly small sack.
flavorText: “Lentils are tiny promises that supper will happen.”

name: rice
shortDescription: Rice grains pale, dry, and easy to measure, valued wherever clean starch can turn sauce, broth, or scraps into a meal. It travels well by sack and turns water, patience, and heat into steady comfort.
flavorText: “Rice is quiet until an empty bowl asks for it.”

name: wheat
shortDescription: Wheat grain ready for milling, trading, or hoarding by anyone who understands bread is politics with a crust. It is plain in the sack but mighty in the ledger, because towns measure safety by flour stores.
flavorText: “Wheat becomes bread, beer, taxes, and trouble in that order.”

name: barley
shortDescription: Barley grain with a hardy reputation, useful for porridge, bread, animal feed, and the brewing vats that make cold evenings tolerable. It grows where fussier crops complain, which makes it beloved by farmers and brewers alike.
flavorText: “Barley is grain with muddy boots and excellent prospects.”
